history.diagnosis: The reward function provides a nearly constant signal (r_progress ≈ 0.39) with no incentive to move, causing the agent to learn a stationary policy that applies maximum force without gaining speed.

The reward function gives a constant high baseline (r_progress ≈ 0.39) with no incentive to move, and a terminal bonus that is incorrectly triggered on timeout (not only on success), allowing the agent to stay stationary and collect reward without ever solving the task.

Agent is stuck applying maximum force with negligible velocity because the reward function provides a constant positive baseline (r_position ≈ 0.39 + constant r_goal) that masks any incentive to move, leading to reward hacking and no task progress.

The agent is stuck in a local optimum with negligible velocity and zero goal attainment because the reward function provides a direction-agnostic velocity bonus that does not incentivize progress toward the goal, and lacks a dense shaping signal to guide the car up the hill.

The agent is stuck in a local optimum with near-zero velocity and zero goal attainment because the reward signal is extremely weak (mean ~0.006 per step), the signed direction reward penalizes leftward movement essential for building momentum, and the distance gradient is too flat to guide the car u

The agent is stuck in a low-velocity stall because the reward signal is negligible (mean ~0.006), with a signed direction reward penalizing leftward movement needed for oscillation, and a velocity bonus too weak to overcome inertia.

The _outcome terminal penalty (-1) dominates the reward signal (~97% of magnitude), forcing the agent to prioritize episode length over task progress — it jitters with high force but negligible net velocity to avoid termination while collecting a tiny positive dense reward.
